notes: Final revised questionnaire seed data. q17-q22 extend the main fields; a01-a08 measure Agency / Action Pull.

questions.text: What do you usually notice first when something feels off?

options.text: A pull to check whether it has something to do with me.
A shift in the people around me.
That something isn't getting the attention it needs.
That something isn't right.

questions.text: What tends to stay with you the longest?

options.text: A question about myself I haven't been able to settle.
A relationship that shifted and never quite recovered.
Something that never got the chance it needed.
Something wrong that nobody ever addressed.

questions.text: When something keeps bothering you, what usually happens next?

options.text: I sit with it until I understand it better.
I keep turning it over until something shifts.
I start thinking about what could actually change it.
I find myself doing something about it before I've decided to.

questions.text: When something needs consistent effort just to stay intact, what do you do?

options.text: Reconnect with why it matters to me personally.
Check in with who's actually carrying the weight of it.
Focus on the small things that need attention right now.
Make sure the effort is being shared fairly.

questions.text: When you sense something important, how does it usually arrive?

options.text: As a vague feeling I can't place yet.
As a thought that quietly keeps returning.
As a pressure that builds until I act.
As something I can't stop turning over.

questions.text: When you try to understand a difficult situation, where do you begin?

options.text: With my own position in it.
With what each person involved was going through.
With what got overlooked or wasn't taken care of.
With what actually happened.

questions.text: When you notice something fragile, what feels most natural?

options.text: Watch and wait to see what it needs.
Stay close without intervening yet.
Find what's missing and try to provide it.
Do something before the moment passes.

questions.text: When you see someone struggling with something they're still learning, what do you do?

options.text: Check whether this is actually what they want to be doing.
Make sure they know their effort is being seen, not just the outcome.
Think about what kind of practice or structure would help them.
Try to remove whatever is getting in the way of them developing.

questions.text: Which feels most important to protect?

options.text: My sense of who I am.
The people I care about.
Something that's still in the middle of becoming.
What's actually true about the situation.

questions.text: What feels most difficult to leave unresolved?

options.text: Feeling like I was misread.
Knowing something real got wasted.
Someone ending up alone with something they shouldn't have had to carry.
Something wrong being left to quietly become normal.

questions.text: When something feels unfair, what do you usually want first?

options.text: To understand what happened and why.
To find the right words for it.
For it to be named out loud by someone.
For something to actually change because of it.

questions.text: When something catastrophic happens, what hits you hardest?

options.text: What it does to your sense of who you are.
How the people around you are reacting to it.
What it means for everyone and how it can be held together.
What people fear it might lead to.

questions.text: How long do subtle signals usually stay with you?

options.text: Not long. They pass on their own.
They drift back sometimes, but loosely.
They stay somewhere in the background, still running.
They keep coming back until I do something about them.

questions.text: Which kind of mistake bothers you most?

options.text: Getting someone completely wrong.
Doing something that cost someone their trust in me.
Failing to look after something while I still could.
Letting something dishonest go without saying anything.

questions.text: When a situation feels stuck, what do you tend to do?

options.text: Wait for something in it to become clearer.
Keep looking for what hasn't been understood yet.
Find one thing that can move and try to shift it.
Push until something gives.

questions.text: When does a familiar place start to feel unsafe to you?

options.text: When you can no longer be yourself there.
When the relationships between the people in it have fractured.
When what used to feel like care starts to feel like control.
When something went wrong but no one acknowledges it.

questions.text: What do you tend to look for beneath the surface?

options.text: Who someone actually is, not just how they're coming across.
What someone actually needs but hasn't said.
Where the tension between people is really coming from.
The real reason something happened.

questions.text: What makes you want to step in?

options.text: Someone is being seen as something they're not.
Something is going to fall apart if no one pays attention to it.
Someone is being failed by the people around them and nobody notices.
Something unfair is starting to just be accepted.

questions.text: When you sense someone isn't being supported, what happens first?

options.text: I pay closer attention.
I make sure they know I'm there if they need me.
I think about what would actually help and offer it.
I do something without waiting to be asked.

questions.text: When something major in your life has ended, what stays with you the longest?

options.text: How it changed you.
How it changed the people around you.
What still needs to be tended before closure can arrive.
What it never got a proper answer for.

questions.text: When others dismiss what you notice, what happens?

options.text: I can usually let it go.
It nags a little, but I move on.
I want it acknowledged, even if nothing changes.
It sits wrong with me in a way that's hard to shake.

questions.text: A situation keeps repeating. What do you most want to know?

options.text: What it keeps revealing about me.
What it's still waiting for.
Who's been quietly carrying it.
Why it keeps happening.

questions.text: When you realise you've been misunderstood, what do you usually do?

options.text: Try to understand first why it landed the way it did.
Wait for the right moment to say something.
Find a way to explain what was actually missed.
Correct it straight away.

questions.text: When an unexpected opportunity arrives, what do you consider first?

options.text: Whether it aligns with where you are and where you're headed.
How it would affect the people around me.
Whether the timing and conditions are right for it.
What it's actually bringing and whether it's safe.

questions.text: Something unfamiliar enters your life. What matters to you first?

options.text: What it might change about me.
Whether it has room to grow.
Whether the people around me can make space for it.
What it's actually bringing with it.

questions.text: When something breaks, what do you first want to recover?

options.text: My own sense of what happened.
The conditions for things to start repairing.
The closeness between the people involved.
A clear picture of what actually went wrong.

questions.text: When a group keeps avoiding the real issue, what feels most like you?

options.text: I notice it and keep watching.
I try to understand why nobody's saying it yet.
I look for a way to bring it up without forcing it.
I say it.

questions.text: When something stays unresolved, how does it feel?

options.text: It fades on its own, given time.
It softens. Still there, but distant.
It sits with me, waiting to find its shape.
It presses. It wants an answer.

questions.text: What kind of unresolved thing feels most alive to you?

options.text: A part of myself I haven't figured out yet.
Something that ended before it could finish becoming.
A relationship that still pulls at me.
A wrong that was never properly dealt with.

questions.text: When something has potential but no clear shape yet, what do you usually do?

options.text: Leave it alone until it shows me something.
Stay with it and see where it wants to go.
Start giving it some structure.
Begin making it into something concrete.
